title: Fennel seed tea for wind and bloating
subtitle: A carminative infusion from the kitchen spice rack
excerpt: A hot infusion of crushed fennel seeds, traditionally taken after a heavy or gassy meal. One of the oldest carminative preparations in the western kitchen, recorded by Maud Grieve and in use well before her.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), fennel entry, carminative action, the after-meal cup, culinary-to-medicinal continuum. Nicholas Culpeper, The English Physician (1652), fennel as a digestive herb. EMA HMPC monograph on Foeniculum vulgare var. dulce (2008), modern dosing and safety reference.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Fennel seeds keep 2 years in a sealed jar away from light. Crush just before brewing, the volatile oils that carry the carminative action dissipate once the seed is broken open. Brew fresh for every cup.

recipeTools.notes: For lightly crushing the seeds. A rolling pin on a board works.
Any small teapot with a lid.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb traditionally taken to ease trapped wind and digestive spasm, typically by relaxing the smooth muscle of the gut wall.
The aromatic oil fraction of a herb that evaporates on heating, the carrier of much of the carminative and aromatic action in seeds and flowers.

content.text: A cup of fennel seed tea after a gassy, bloating meal. Fennel is the kitchen's oldest after-dinner carminative, recorded in Maud Grieve's Modern Herbal (1931), in Culpeper's English Physician (1652), and in domestic medicine books across Europe going back centuries before either. The preparation is as simple as a cup of tea: a teaspoon of lightly crushed fennel seeds in a lidded pot, just-off-boil water poured over, steeped covered for ten minutes. The lid is the rule, the
volatile oils
that carry the carminative action are heat-sensitive; without the lid they escape as steam.
Fennel belongs to the Apiaceae family alongside caraway, anise, and dill, the whole family shares the
carminative

character. The active constituent is anethole, the compound that gives fennel its anise-like smell and its traditional action on wind, colic, and mild spasm in the gut. The EMA's herbal monograph on fennel confirms its traditional use for mild digestive complaints in adults.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Allergy: fennel is in the Apiaceae family, which cross-reacts with celery, carrot, parsley, and coriander. Readers with a known allergy to any of these should avoid fennel preparations or patch-test (dilute cold infusion on the forearm) before using internally.

Pregnancy: culinary use of fennel seeds in cooking is unproblematic. Therapeutic-dose fennel teas and especially concentrated tinctures are avoided in pregnancy because the herb has weak oestrogenic activity and has traditionally been used as an emmenagogue. A cup of weak fennel tea after a meal is the low end of the dose range; a strong daily therapeutic preparation is not recommended without advice from a herbalist or midwife.

Breastfeeding: fennel is traditionally cited as a galactagogue (milk-support herb) and is widely used in nursing teas across Europe. The safety profile at culinary doses is considered acceptable; however, regular therapeutic-dose use in breastfeeding should be discussed with a midwife. Very high doses in animal studies have raised oestrogenicity concerns that have not translated into reported problems at normal dietary or tea-strength doses.

See your doctor if bloating is present every day for more than three weeks, comes with weight loss or a change in bowel habit, or is accompanied by pain that does not ease after passing wind.

content.text: What you need

items.prepNote: about 1 teaspoon, lightly crush with a pestle or rolling pin just before brewing
optional, to taste, add once below 60°C

content.text: The standard ratio is 1 teaspoon (about 5 g) of seeds per 250 ml cup, roughly 1 part seed to 50 parts water by weight. Lightly crushing the seeds exposes the volatile oils and makes for a stronger cup without requiring a longer steep.
Method
Crush the seeds

Place {{fennel-seeds}} of fennel seeds in a pestle and mortar and give them three or four firm presses, enough to crack the seed coat and expose the white interior, not enough to pulverise them to powder. The smell should be immediate and strongly anise-like; if it is faint, the seeds are old and the cup will be weak. A rolling pin pressed firmly over seeds on a board does the same job.
Steep covered, 10 minutes

Put the crushed seeds into a teapot or a tea infuser in a cup. Boil the kettle and wait 30 seconds, the water should be around 95°C, not at a rolling boil. Pour {{water}} over the seeds and put the lid on immediately. Set a timer for 10 minutes. The lid traps the volatile oils; without it the most active part of the tea is already in the kitchen ceiling.
Strain and sip

Strain through the infuser or a fine sieve. The cup should smell distinctly of anise and be pale yellow-gold. Add honey only if the bitterness is a problem, most readers prefer fennel tea without sweetener. Drink slowly while still warm, over 10, 15 minutes, after the meal that caused the discomfort.
Dosing

One cup after the meal that caused the problem. Up to three cups a day during an acute episode, morning, afternoon, and evening, for adults. The traditional course is short: a cup or two until the discomfort settles. For chronic or daily bloating, the home cup is not the answer, see your GP to understand why the gut is doing what it is doing.

Children: not recommended in infants under six months. For older infants and toddlers, fennel is commonly used in continental European traditions as a very dilute gripe water, but always at herbalist or GP supervision, adult dosing divided by weight is not safe paediatric dosing. For children over eight, half an adult cup of weak tea after a meal is the traditional limit.
Storage

Fennel seeds keep 2 years in a sealed glass jar or tin away from light and heat. Whole seeds retain their volatile oils best, crush only what you will use immediately. Brewed tea does not keep; make each cup fresh.
When NOT to use this remedy

Skip fennel tea if you have a known allergy to celery, carrot, parsley, or other Apiaceae plants. Skip during pregnancy at therapeutic-dose levels (see safety notes above). See your doctor for bloating that is present every day for three weeks or more, that comes with unexplained weight loss, a change in bowel habit, rectal bleeding, or that wakes you at night.

attrs.heading: If the cup fell flat
attrs.intro: Two common reasons fennel tea disappoints.

items.symptom: Tea smells faintly of anise but the flavour is thin
items.cause: Seeds are old, the volatile oils have dissipated from the seed coat.
items.fix: Smell the seeds before crushing. Fresh fennel seeds smell strongly of anise when the seed coat is scratched. If the smell is faint at this stage, buy fresh stock. Use a whole teaspoon, not a heaped quarter-teaspoon.

items.cause: Steeped too long, or water was too hot, drawing out tannins.
items.fix: Stop at 10 minutes and use water that has sat 30 seconds off the boil. A small amount of honey balances the bitterness.
